name: Inventory Limit
description: Inventory space, weight, slots, or containers limit how much the player can carry during survival play.
design_notes: Inventory limits are strongest when they create choices rather than clerical cleanup. The design should define slot capacity, weight capacity, stack rules, overflow behavior, encumbrance thresholds, and how crafting or gathering transactions fail safely when storage is full.
design_purpose: Force prioritization between food, water, tools, ammunition, materials, and valuables.
Make bases, containers, vehicles, companions, or upgrades valuable as storage solutions.
Connect gathering and crafting to route planning by limiting what can be carried at once.
player_fantasy: The player feels like every item has weight in their survival plan and must choose what matters.

parameters.name: inventory_container_id
parameters.description: Container identifier used for capacity checks, ownership, persistence, and UI display.

parameters.name: slot_capacity_count
parameters.description: Maximum occupied slots allowed in the container before slot-based transactions fail.

parameters.name: weight_capacity_kg
parameters.description: Maximum carried weight before encumbrance, rejection, or movement penalties apply.

parameters.name: stack_limit_rule_id
parameters.description: Rule that decides how identical or compatible item instances combine into stacks.

parameters.name: encumbrance_threshold_id
parameters.description: Threshold table that maps carried load into warnings and movement penalties.

parameters.name: overflow_policy_id
parameters.description: Rule used when gathering, looting, or crafting would exceed container capacity.

edge_cases: Crafting consumes several ingredients and produces an output that no longer fits the container.
A player picks up a stackable item while another transaction splits or moves the same stack.
Weight modifiers from equipment, hunger, injuries, or companions update while inventory UI is open.
A container is destroyed, moved, or loses ownership while holding player items.
common_bugs: Capacity validation runs after item pickup, creating negative slots or overweight states that cannot be resolved.
Stack splitting loses item_instance_id metadata such as durability, quality, or ownership.
Server and client sort inventory differently, causing drag transactions to move the wrong slot.
Encumbrance penalties apply from total item definitions but ignore per-instance stack counts.
balancing_notes: Strict limits make every expedition strategic, but excessive shuffling can become the dominant play activity.
Storage upgrades should reduce friction without eliminating the need to choose route goals.
Overflow behavior should be consistent; surprise item drops can feel like data loss.
accessibility_notes: Provide sortable lists, readable capacity text, and controller-friendly slot navigation.
Use warnings before rejecting or dropping items so players understand capacity failures.

implementation_notes.unity: Use transaction validation for every pickup, craft, split, and move operation. Store inventory_container_id, slot coordinates, stack counts, item_instance_id metadata, and weight totals; never let UI drag state mutate inventory without service approval.
implementation_notes.godot: Keep inventory as data and let GridContainer or Control nodes render a snapshot. Slot moves should submit source, destination, expected item_instance_id, and stack count so stale UI cannot corrupt capacity state.
implementation_notes.unreal: A replicated inventory component should own slot and weight validation on the server. Client drag operations can predict visuals, but the server must confirm capacity, stack rules, ownership, and encumbrance before finalizing.
implementation_notes.web: Represent containers as arrays or maps with stable item_instance_id entries. For browser saves, persist slot positions, counts, and metadata together; for multiplayer, treat drag and stack split as versioned transactions.

example_games.title: Resident Evil 4
example_games.note: Uses limited inventory space as a major planning and item-management constraint.

example_games.title: The Elder Scrolls V: Skyrim
example_games.note: Uses carry weight to limit looting and travel decisions.

example_games.title: Minecraft
example_games.note: Uses limited inventory slots to constrain gathering, crafting, and travel.

example_games.title: Subnautica
example_games.note: Uses limited inventory space to constrain long exploration and resource trips.

example_games.title: Terraria
example_games.note: Uses limited inventory slots to constrain mining, crafting, and exploration trips.
